headteacher.definition: The person in charge of a school (British term for principal).
headteacher.example: The headteacher gave a talk to all the new parents.

secondary school.definition: A school for children aged about 11-18 (after primary school).
secondary school.example: Her daughter started secondary school this year.

stonking.definition: Very big or very great (informal British).
stonking.example: Don't make a stonking big deal of it when your child lies.

trivial.definition: Not important; small and not worth worrying about.
trivial.example: The thing she did was relatively trivial.

sin.definition: An act that is considered morally wrong.
sin.example: Parents seem to put lying above all other sins.

sinners.definition: People who have done something morally wrong.
sinners.example: We shouldn't treat children like the greatest sinners.

intimacy.definition: A feeling of being very close to someone emotionally.
intimacy.example: Not lying gives us a better chance for real intimacy.

pretend.definition: To act as if something is true when it is not.
pretend.example: We tell children to pretend to be grateful for gifts.

grateful.example: She pretended to be grateful for the ugly scarf.

scarf.definition: A piece of cloth worn around the neck for warmth or style.
scarf.example: Granny knitted an ugly scarf for Christmas.

knitted.definition: Made from yarn or thread using needles to loop together.
knitted.example: Granny knitted them scarves every year.

witness.example: Children witness their parents telling lies all the time.

overheard.definition: Heard something that was not meant for you to hear.
overheard.example: The child overheard his parents telling a lie.

reception.definition: A formal party or social event.
reception.example: They didn't want to go to the work reception.

persuade.definition: To make someone believe or do something by talking to them.
persuade.example: She had the power to persuade people that the truth was different.

accomplished.definition: Skilful; showing a high level of ability.
accomplished.example: Lying is an accomplished thing for a child to do.

conceive.definition: To form an idea or thought in your mind.
conceive.example: They have to conceive of an alternative reality.

alternative.example: The child must imagine an alternative version of events.

distinguish.definition: To see or understand the difference between two things.
distinguish.example: They must distinguish between what is real and what is made up.

superpower.definition: An amazing ability, like those of a superhero.
superpower.example: The child felt like lying was a new superpower.

deceitful.definition: Deliberately trying to make someone believe something false.
deceitful.example: A toddler may do deceitful things like feeding the dog behind your back.

indulge.definition: To allow yourself or someone to do something enjoyable.
indulge.example: A toddler may indulge in deceitful behaviour.

unjudgemental.definition: Not forming negative opinions about people.
unjudgemental.example: Adults should be calm and unjudgemental about the truth.

fantasy.definition: Something that exists only in the imagination.
fantasy.example: Some children tell fantasy lies to make sense of feelings.

narrative.definition: A story or description of events.
narrative.example: The child invented a narrative to fit her feelings.

nursery school.definition: A school for very young children, usually ages 3-5.
nursery school.example: She told a lie at nursery school about her goldfish.

bouncy.definition: Happy, energetic, and full of life.
bouncy.example: Flo did not seem her usual bouncy self.

goldfish.definition: A small orange fish often kept as a pet.
goldfish.example: She said her goldfish had died, but they never had one.

bereavement.definition: The sadness and pain after someone you love dies.
bereavement.example: She could not handle the enormity of my bereavement.

enormity.definition: The very large size or seriousness of something.
enormity.example: The child could not take in the enormity of the situation.

self-soothing.definition: Making yourself feel calm and better on your own.
self-soothing.example: The fantasy lie was a form of self-soothing.

aberrant.definition: Different from what is normal or expected, in a bad way.
aberrant.example: Look to understand the feelings behind aberrant behaviour.

condemn.definition: To say strongly that something is wrong or bad.
condemn.example: Don't condemn the child for the behaviour.

predicted.definition: Said that something would happen before it did.
predicted.example: As you may have predicted, the headteacher's words came true.

tragedy.definition: A very sad or terrible event.
tragedy.example: I did not treat this lie as the greatest tragedy.

descend.definition: To come down; to happen to someone.
descend.example: It was not the greatest tragedy to descend upon me.

revising.definition: Studying again to prepare for an exam (British English).
revising.example: They said they were revising at each other's house.

union.definition: An organization of students or workers.
union.example: They went to the university's student union bar.

entitled.definition: Having the right to do or have something.
entitled.example: They were not entitled to use the private bar.

escapades.example: Looking back on my own escapades, I was lucky.

sophisticated.definition: Experienced and having knowledge of the world; worldly.
sophisticated.example: She tried to impress people who seemed more sophisticated.

cunning.example: It was a cunning trick question on my part.

sensible.definition: Showing good thinking; practical and reasonable.

festival.definition: A large event with music, food, and entertainment.
festival.example: I let her camp at a pop festival with her friends.

marvellous.example: It was a marvellous adventure for a pair of sixteen-year-olds.

overreact.definition: To respond more strongly than is necessary.
overreact.example: Try not to overreact to what your child tells you.

containment.definition: The act of holding or managing strong feelings safely.
containment.example: She veered too far from the ideal of containment.

veering.definition: Turning away from the direction you should go.
veering.example: She was veering too far from being calm.

erred.example: I had perhaps erred by overreacting.

deemed.definition: Considered or judged to be something.
deemed.example: She deemed me unready to hear the story.

straining.definition: Pushing hard against limits or restrictions.
straining.example: Teenagers are straining at the restrictions their parents put in place.

restrictions.definition: Rules that limit what you can do.
restrictions.example: Parents put restrictions in place out of fear.

forge.definition: To create or build something new, often with effort.
forge.example: Teenagers need privacy to forge their own identity.

privacy.definition: The state of being alone and not watched or disturbed by others.
privacy.example: Teenagers need privacy to grow up.

spooked.example: The child felt spooked by the instructor's behaviour.

overtones.definition: Hidden extra meanings or feelings beyond what is obvious.
overtones.example: She felt spooked by the sexual overtones.

sounding board.definition: A person you talk to in order to test your ideas.
sounding board.example: Be a sounding board rather than always telling them what to do.

oracle.definition: A person who gives wise answers to all questions.
oracle.example: If you are a sounding board rather than an oracle, they will keep talking to you.

instil.definition: To gradually put an idea or feeling into someone's mind.
instil.example: Hold back so that you instil confidence in your child.

disempowering.definition: Making someone feel they have no power or ability.
disempowering.example: Always giving answers is disempowering for a child.

moralistic.definition: Telling people what is right and wrong in a preachy way.
moralistic.example: A moralistic approach does not make children more truthful.

punitive.definition: Meant to punish; harsh in punishment.
punitive.example: The punitive school created better liars.

regime.definition: A system of rules or a way things are managed.
regime.example: The two schools had very different disciplinary regimes.

misdemeanours.definition: Small wrongdoings or bad behaviours that are not very serious.

detention.definition: A punishment where a student must stay at school after classes end.
detention.example: You might get guidance and a detention.

peeping.definition: Looking secretly at something you are not supposed to see.
peeping.example: She did an experiment called the peeping game.

convincingly.definition: In a way that makes people believe it is true.

unwittingly.example: The school had unwittingly created great liars.

cracking down.definition: Using stricter rules or harsher punishment.
cracking down.example: Cracking down on lying made the children lie better.

developmental.definition: Related to the process of growing and learning.
developmental.example: Lying is a developmental stage for children.

confiding.definition: Sharing secret or personal information with someone you trust.
confiding.example: Being too strict will stop your child confiding in you.

approval.definition: The feeling that someone thinks you are good or right.
approval.example: Children want to get their parents' approval.

draconian.definition: Very harsh and strict, often unfairly so.
draconian.example: A draconian regime does not turn out good citizens.

jeopardize.definition: To put something at risk of being damaged or lost.
jeopardize.example: Harsh parenting may jeopardize their ability to form relationships.

sustaining.definition: Lasting and giving strength or support over time.

pupil.example: Margaret told me about a pupil of hers.

earthquake.definition: A sudden shaking of the ground caused by movements in the earth.
earthquake.example: She claimed to have a relative in every earthquake.

sympathy.definition: Feeling sorry for someone and understanding their sadness.
sympathy.example: She invented stories to get attention and sympathy.

roundabout.definition: Not direct; going a long way around.
roundabout.example: The child took a roundabout route to get sympathy.
